q: Which file extension does Bitwig Studio use for its native project files?
opts: .bwproject
.bwp
.bitwigproject
.bwsession
explain: Bitwig Studio saves complete projects as .bwproject files. This format contains the full project state including automation, MIDI data, and references to audio files.

q: Which file format does Bitwig Studio use to store automation curve shapes that can be loaded into the Segments modulator?
opts: .bwproject
.bwpreset
.bwcurve
.multisample
explain: .bwcurve files store automation curve shapes and in Bitwig 6 are directly integrated with the Segments (MSEG) modulator, allowing automation clips to be saved and reused as modulator shapes.

q: Which track type in Bitwig Studio can contain both audio input and an instrument device chain, outputting only audio?
opts: Audio Track
Instrument Track
Hybrid Track
Group Track
explain: The Hybrid Track accepts both live audio input and instrument devices simultaneously, making it ideal for processing live audio through instrument-style device chains while outputting audio to the mix.

q: Bitwig Studio's Controller API, used to write custom hardware controller scripts, is based on which programming language?
opts: Python
JavaScript
Lua
Java
explain: Bitwig's Controller API uses JavaScript for writing custom hardware controller scripts. Scripts are stored in the user's Bitwig Studio/Controller Scripts directory and provide full access to Bitwig's host API.

q: What does 'Bounce In Place' do to a selected MIDI clip in Bitwig Studio's Arranger?
opts: Exports the clip as a WAV file to disk
Renders the clip and all device chain processing into a new audio clip at the same position on the same track
Consolidates all clips on the track into one region
Creates a frozen duplicate of the clip on a new Audio track
explain: Bounce In Place renders the selected clip including all instrument and FX chain processing into a new audio clip at the same position on the same track, replacing the original clip.

q: What is the purpose of Take Lanes in Bitwig Studio?
opts: To store multiple automation versions for a single parameter
To layer multiple instrument devices on a single track
To record and comp multiple takes of an audio or MIDI performance on the same track
To create parallel processing chains within the Arranger
explain: Take Lanes appear below a track when loop-recording, storing each pass as a separate take. You can then comp the best sections from different takes into a final performance.

q: What is the key difference between 'Time Selection' mode and 'Object Selection' mode in Bitwig Studio's Arranger?
opts: Time Selection only works on audio clips; Object Selection works on MIDI clips
Time Selection creates a range across all tracks regardless of clip boundaries; Object Selection targets individual clip objects
Time Selection locks the BPM; Object Selection allows tempo automation
Object Selection enables multi-track editing; Time Selection is single-track only
explain: Time Selection (range selection) creates a horizontal time range across all tracks useful for insertions, deletions, and ripple edits, while Object Selection targets individual clips and objects as discrete entities.

q: When extracting groove from an audio clip in Bitwig Studio, what data does Bitwig analyze to generate the groove template?
opts: The clip's embedded MIDI note positions
The transient positions detected in the audio waveform
The stretch markers manually placed by the user
The quantization grid applied during recording
explain: Groove extraction analyzes transient positions within an audio clip to capture the timing feel of the performance. This template can then be applied to MIDI clips or other audio for humanization.

q: In Bitwig Studio's signal flow, where are Note FX devices processed relative to the instrument?
opts: After the instrument, before the audio FX
After all audio FX, before the fader
Before the instrument, processing note data
In parallel with the instrument
explain: Note FX devices sit before the instrument in the signal chain, processing MIDI/note data (pitch, velocity, timing) before it reaches the instrument for sound generation. The full signal flow is: Note FX → Instrument → Audio FX → Fader → Pan → Sends → Output.

q: In Bitwig Studio, how is send/return routing implemented?
opts: Via dedicated Send tracks with a built-in return bus
By routing audio from any track to an Effect (FX) track using Send devices
Through the Mixer's auxiliary channels, separate from the main track list
Using the Audio Receiver device placed on a Group track
explain: Send routing in Bitwig uses Effect (FX) tracks as return busses. A Send device on a source track feeds audio to the FX track at a configurable level, supporting both pre-fader and post-fader configurations.

q: What happens when an audio effect device is set to 'Pre' fader mode in Bitwig Studio?
opts: The device processes audio before it reaches any other devices in the chain
The device's output level is unaffected by the track fader
The device is bypassed until the fader reaches 0 dB
The device processes audio after all Post-fader devices
explain: A Pre-fader device processes audio independently of the track fader, meaning fader automation does not affect the signal level feeding that device — useful for level-sensitive dynamics processors like compressors or limiters.

q: What is the primary use case for Bitwig Studio's Audio Receiver device?
opts: To record audio from an external hardware instrument into the project
To tap audio from any other track's output and route it into the current track's device chain for parallel processing
To receive audio from a ReWire-connected application
To monitor audio interface input without latency compensation
explain: The Audio Receiver device taps the post-fader output of any other track and injects it into the current track's device chain, enabling complex parallel processing topologies without needing dedicated send/return infrastructure.

q: Which Bitwig Studio built-in instrument uses drawbar-style additive synthesis modeled after a tonewheel organ?
opts: Polysynth
Organ
Polymer
Phase-4
explain: Organ is Bitwig's additive drawbar synthesizer, modeling the harmonic structure of tonewheel organs with adjustable drawbar levels for each harmonic partial.

q: What makes Bitwig's Polymer synthesizer architecturally unique compared to other built-in instruments?
opts: It is the only instrument that natively supports MPE polyphonic expression
Its oscillator, filter, and envelope components are swappable Grid-compatible modules
It uses a 4-operator FM matrix identical to classic DX-series synthesizers
It is the only instrument capable of loading external VST instruments as modules
explain: Polymer is a hybrid modular synthesizer where core components (oscillator, filter, amp, envelopes) are interchangeable Grid modules, allowing extensive customization of the synthesis architecture beyond fixed-architecture instruments.

q: What is the functional difference between Mono and Legato voice modes in Bitwig instruments?
opts: Legato retriggers the envelope on each new note; Mono does not
Mono retriggers the envelope on each new note; Legato slides pitch without retriggering when a key is held
Legato allows two simultaneous voices; Mono allows only one
There is no functional difference; they are aliases for the same mode
explain: In Mono mode, each new note retriggers the envelope. In Legato mode, if a key is held when a new note is played, the pitch changes (with portamento if configured) but the envelope continues without retriggering.

q: How does MPE (MIDI Polyphonic Expression) work in Bitwig Studio?
opts: MPE data is received on a single MIDI channel and routed to all voices simultaneously
Each note voice receives its own MIDI channel carrying independent pitch bend, pressure, and slide data
MPE requires a dedicated MPE track type separate from standard Instrument tracks
Bitwig converts MPE data to standard MIDI CC before passing it to instruments
explain: MPE assigns each note to its own MIDI channel, enabling per-note pitch bend, pressure (channel aftertouch), and slide (CC74) as independent expression per voice. Bitwig exposes this via Expressions modulators.

q: Which Grid device type should you use to build a polyphonic synthesizer where each voice has its own independent signal path?
opts: Mono Grid
FX Grid
Poly Grid
Note Grid
explain: Poly Grid creates a polyphonic instrument where each voice runs its own independent instance of the signal path, allowing per-voice modulation and processing. Mono Grid is strictly monophonic.

q: In The Grid's pitch convention, what voltage value represents the note C3?
opts: 1V
0V
3V
explain: The Grid uses a 1V/octave pitch standard where 0V = C3. Each octave up adds 1V (C4 = 1V, C5 = 2V) and each octave down subtracts 1V (C2 = -1V).

q: What is required to create a stable feedback loop in The Grid without causing infinite gain buildup?
opts: A DC Offset module to re-center the signal
A Delay module (Short Delay or Long Delay) inserted in the feedback path
A Limiter module at the feedback return point
Feedback loops are not supported in The Grid
explain: The Grid requires at least one sample of delay in any feedback path to prevent zero-latency feedback loops. Inserting a Short Delay or Long Delay breaks the instantaneous loop and allows stable feedback processing.

q: In a Poly Grid, what determines whether a signal path runs per-voice or as a shared monophonic signal?
opts: All modules in a Poly Grid always run per-voice
Signals sourced from per-voice sources (like Note In) run per-voice; signals from shared sources (like Audio In or global modulators) are shared across all voices
Only oscillator modules run per-voice; all other modules are shared
Each module is explicitly assigned to per-voice or shared mode via a context menu
explain: In a Poly Grid, modules connected downstream of per-voice sources (Note In for pitch/gate) run independently per voice, while modules connected only to shared sources (Audio In, global LFOs) process a single shared signal.

q: What happens automatically when you connect an audio-rate signal to a module input that operates at control rate in The Grid?
opts: The connection is rejected and shown with a red error indicator
Bitwig automatically downsamples the audio-rate signal to control rate at the connection point
The module switches to audio-rate processing, increasing CPU usage
The signal passes through unchanged, potentially causing aliasing
explain: The Grid automatically converts between audio-rate and control-rate signals at connection points. When an audio-rate signal feeds a control-rate input it is downsampled transparently — no manual intervention is required.

q: How do you assign a modulator to a specific parameter in Bitwig Studio?
opts: Drag the modulator from the Browser onto the target parameter
Right-click the parameter and select 'Assign Modulator'
Click the modulator's output connector, then click (and drag) the target parameter to set depth
Enable 'Modulation Mode' in preferences, then drag from modulator to parameter
explain: To assign a modulator, click the modulator's output dot to enter assignment mode — all modulatable parameters become highlighted. Click and drag on the target parameter to assign it and set the modulation depth simultaneously.

q: What does 'recursive modulation' mean in Bitwig Studio's modulation system?
opts: A modulator that feeds its own output back into itself to create self-oscillation
A modulator whose own parameters (such as Rate or Depth) are themselves modulated by another modulator
Modulation that loops back from the instrument output to the Note FX chain
Using a modulator inside a nested device container
explain: Recursive (chained) modulation means one modulator's parameters are controlled by another modulator — for example, an LFO's Rate being modulated by an Envelope Follower for dynamic, expressive modulation behavior.

q: What is the functional difference between Absolute and Relative modulation modes for a modulator assignment in Bitwig?
opts: Absolute modulation uses fixed voltage ranges; Relative modulation scales to the parameter's current range
In Absolute mode, modulation targets a fixed value range; in Relative mode, it offsets from the parameter's current knob position
Relative mode only works with bipolar modulators; Absolute mode works with all types
The modes only affect display, not actual behavior
explain: In Relative mode, modulation is added as an offset to the parameter's knob value, so adjusting the knob shifts the entire modulation range. In Absolute mode, the modulation operates on a fixed value range regardless of the knob position.

q: When using the Macro device in Bitwig Studio, what capability distinguishes it from a simple one-to-many Remote Control assignment?
opts: The Macro device can control parameters on tracks other than its host track
Each mapped parameter has its own independent response curve and min/max range, enabling non-linear and inverted mappings
The Macro device supports MIDI learn for hardware controller assignment, unlike Remote Controls
Macro mappings persist when the preset is saved and loaded in a different project
explain: The Macro device maps one knob to many parameters, but each individual mapping has its own curve shape and independent min/max range. This enables complex relationships like a single macro that opens one filter while simultaneously closing another.

q: Where in an Instrument track's device chain must Note FX devices be placed?
opts: After the instrument, in the audio FX section
Before the instrument, in the dedicated Note FX section at the start of the chain
Anywhere — Bitwig identifies Note FX by device type regardless of position
On a separate, dedicated Note FX send track
explain: Note FX devices must be placed in the Note FX section at the beginning of the device chain, before the instrument. They process MIDI/note data and cannot process audio.

q: What does the Rate parameter in Bitwig Studio's Arpeggiator Note FX control?
opts: The speed of pitch transposition between arpeggio notes
The rhythmic subdivision at which arpeggio notes are triggered
The number of octaves the arpeggio spans
The velocity curve applied to each arpeggiated note
explain: The Rate parameter sets the rhythmic subdivision (e.g., 1/8, 1/16, 1/4T) that determines how quickly the arpeggio steps through notes in sync with the project tempo.

q: In Bitwig Studio 6's Key Filter+ device, what does 'Constrain' mode do differently from 'Filter' mode?
opts: Constrain silences out-of-scale notes; Filter transposes them to the nearest scale tone
Filter blocks out-of-scale notes entirely; Constrain shifts them to the nearest scale degree without silencing
Constrain applies to velocity; Filter applies to pitch
Filter works in real-time; Constrain only applies during MIDI export
explain: Filter mode silences notes that fall outside the selected scale, while Constrain mode keeps all notes playing but transposes any out-of-scale note to the nearest scale degree, preserving rhythmic density.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Note Receiver device in Bitwig Studio?
opts: To receive MIDI input from an external hardware controller on a specific channel
To route note/MIDI data generated by another track into the current track's Note FX chain
To convert audio transients into MIDI trigger notes
To merge MIDI from multiple hardware ports into a single instrument track
explain: Note Receiver routes note data from any other track's instrument or clips into the current track's Note FX chain, enabling cross-track MIDI routing — for instance, driving multiple instruments from a single MIDI clip.

q: In Bitwig Studio's Clip Launcher, what does triggering a Scene do?
opts: It saves the current clip state as a named snapshot
It simultaneously triggers all clips in the same horizontal row across all tracks
It switches the Arranger to a predefined section
It applies the same Follow Action to all clips in the project
explain: A Scene represents a horizontal row of clips in the Clip Launcher. Triggering a scene simultaneously launches all clips in that row across all tracks, enabling instant full-arrangement transitions in live performance.

q: What does the Follow Action 'Next' do in Bitwig Studio's Clip Launcher?
opts: The clip loops indefinitely until manually stopped
After the clip completes, it automatically triggers the clip slot directly below it in the same track column
The clip triggers the next scene across all tracks
After playback, the transport follows the Arranger timeline position
explain: The 'Next' Follow Action causes a clip to automatically trigger the clip slot immediately below it in the same track when it finishes playing, enabling automated upward clip progression without manual triggering.

q: What does the Launch Quantize setting control in Bitwig Studio's Clip Launcher?
opts: The quantization applied to MIDI notes within clips when recorded
The rhythmic grid boundary at which a triggered clip will actually begin playing
The number of bars a clip waits before looping
The tempo quantization applied during scene transitions
explain: Launch Quantize sets when a triggered clip actually starts. Set to 1 bar, clicking a clip trigger waits until the next bar boundary before the clip begins, ensuring all clips stay rhythmically aligned.

q: What does the Follow Action 'Other' do in Bitwig Studio's Clip Launcher?
opts: It repeats the current clip from the beginning
It stops playback of the current clip entirely
It randomly triggers one of the other clips in the same track column, excluding the currently playing clip
It triggers the corresponding clip in the next scene row down
explain: 'Other' randomly selects and triggers any clip in the same track column except the currently playing clip, creating non-repeating random variation — useful for generative live performance without immediate repetition.

q: What voltage standard does Bitwig Studio's HW CV Out device use for pitch when interfacing with Eurorack modular synthesizers?
opts: Hz/V (Hertz per Volt), proportional to oscillator frequency
1V/octave, where each additional volt raises pitch by one octave
5V/octave, matching the Buchla standard
0–10V linear, where 0V is the lowest note and 10V is the highest
explain: Bitwig's HW CV Out uses the 1V/octave standard universal to Eurorack systems. This matches The Grid's internal pitch convention (0V = C3), ensuring seamless integration with Eurorack hardware.

q: What unique capability does the HW Instrument device provide that HW CV Out alone cannot?
opts: It sends polyphonic CV to hardware with MPE-style per-channel routing
It sends MIDI or CV/gate to external hardware AND returns the hardware's audio into Bitwig with automatic latency compensation
It provides analog clock synchronization to hardware sequencers
It supports bidirectional CV — receiving CV from hardware as a modulation source
explain: HW Instrument is a complete hardware instrument integration device: it sends MIDI or CV/gate to external hardware and simultaneously returns its audio output back into Bitwig's device chain with automatic round-trip latency compensation.

q: Why must an audio interface be DC-coupled to use Bitwig Studio's CV output features with Eurorack modular gear?
opts: DC-coupled interfaces support higher sample rates required for CV timing accuracy
Standard AC-coupled interfaces block steady-state voltage components that CV signals require for accurate pitch and gate information
DC coupling provides lower latency for hardware synchronization
Eurorack requires balanced TRS connections which only DC-coupled interfaces support
explain: CV signals include DC (0 Hz) components — a held pitch is a constant voltage. AC-coupled interfaces use capacitors that block DC, causing sustained CV voltages to drift to zero and destroying pitch accuracy.

q: In Bitwig Studio's HW Clock Out device, what does the Pulses Per Quarter Note (PPQN) setting determine?
opts: The audio buffer size used for hardware synchronization calculations
How many analog clock pulses are sent per quarter note, determining resolution and compatibility with external hardware
The number of MIDI clock messages sent per bar to external devices
The voltage amplitude of each clock pulse sent to hardware
explain: PPQN sets how many analog clock pulses Bitwig sends per quarter note. Different hardware expects different values (e.g., 24 PPQN for MIDI-synced gear, 1 PPQN for step sequencers that advance one step per pulse).

q: Which Sampler time-stretch mode in Bitwig Studio changes playback pitch by altering the playback speed, like a tape machine?
opts: Stretch
Cycles
Textures
Repitch
explain: Repitch mode links pitch directly to playback speed — slowing down lowers the pitch, speeding up raises it. No time-stretching algorithm is applied, exactly like a tape machine or record player.

q: In Bitwig Sampler's Textures time-stretch mode, what does the Spray parameter control?
opts: The number of simultaneous grain streams produced
The randomization of grain source position around the current playhead
The amplitude envelope shape applied to each grain
The crossfade time between consecutive grains
explain: Spray randomizes where each grain is sourced from around the current playhead position. Higher Spray values scatter grains further from the exact playhead location, creating more diffuse, cloud-like textural results.

q: In Bitwig's Sampler, what do velocity layers in a multisample allow you to define?
opts: Different playback speeds for different MIDI velocity values
Different audio samples triggered based on how hard a note is played
Velocity-dependent pitch shifting applied to a single sample
Separate send levels per velocity range
explain: Velocity layers map different audio samples to different velocity ranges within the same pitch zone. For example, a piano multisample might use soft recordings for velocity 1–64 and hard recordings for 65–127.

q: What is the primary sonic characteristic of Bitwig Sampler's Cycles time-stretch mode, and for what content is it best suited?
opts: Spectral processing ideal for complex polyphonic audio like full mixes
Waveform cycle preservation during stretching, best suited for monophonic pitched content like single oscillator synth sounds
Random-length granularization, best for percussive material
Formant preservation making it ideal for vocal content at any stretch ratio
explain: Cycles mode analyzes and preserves the periodic cycle structure of the waveform during stretching, producing very clean results for simple monophonic pitched sounds. It creates audible artifacts on complex or polyphonic material.

q: In Bitwig Studio 6, what is an Automation Clip?
opts: A MIDI clip that contains only expression data without note events
A self-contained clip that holds automation data, which can be looped, stretched, and saved independently
An audio clip with automatic pitch correction applied
A clip that automatically adapts its length to match the project tempo
explain: Automation Clips in Bitwig 6 are dedicated clip objects on automation lanes that contain only automation data. They can be looped, time-stretched, and saved as .bwcurve files for reuse across projects.

q: What is the defining behavior of Clip Aliases in Bitwig Studio 6?
opts: They are renamed copies of clips stored in the Browser for quick access
Multiple aliases share one underlying pattern — editing any alias updates all aliases simultaneously
Aliases are read-only reference clips that cannot be edited in the Detail Editor
Aliases store only clip launch settings without audio or MIDI content
explain: Clip Aliases are linked references to a single shared pattern. Editing any alias immediately updates all other aliases, enabling powerful pattern reuse where a single edit propagates to every instance in the arrangement.

q: In Bitwig Studio 6's automation system, what does the Spread feature do to an automation point?
opts: It copies the automation point value to multiple tracks simultaneously
It applies a per-point randomized value range around the point's value, reproducible via a Seed value
It stretches the automation curve symmetrically around the selected point
It distributes the value evenly between neighboring automation points
explain: Spread adds controlled randomization per automation point — each playback, the value is randomly offset within the defined Spread range. The Seed value makes this reproducible, enabling repeatable 'humanized' automation.

q: In Bitwig Studio 6, what does pressing [A] in the Arranger activate?
opts: The Audio Engine settings panel
Automation Mode, overlaying all parameter automation lanes across all tracks
The Audition tool for previewing clips without affecting transport
A new automation clip at the current playhead position
explain: Pressing [A] toggles Automation Mode in Bitwig 6's Arranger, which overlays automation lanes for all parameters across all tracks simultaneously — providing a comprehensive automation view without navigating track by track.

q: In Bitwig Studio 6, how does the Hold automation point type differ from a standard interpolated automation point?
opts: Hold points are fixed at 0 dB and serve as reference anchors
A Hold point maintains a flat, constant value from its position until the next automation point, creating stepped automation
Hold points freeze the parameter at the last manually set value, ignoring subsequent automation
They hold the automation value during playback but allow real-time hardware controller override
explain: Hold points create stepped automation — the value stays constant from the Hold point until the next point, then jumps immediately with no interpolation. This is ideal for discrete parameter changes like preset switches or snapshots.

q: Which third-party plugin formats does Bitwig Studio support?
opts: VST2, VST3, AU, and CLAP
VST2, VST3, and CLAP only
VST3 and CLAP only
VST2, AU, and CLAP only
explain: Bitwig Studio supports VST2, VST3, and CLAP formats. Notably, it does NOT support Audio Units (AU) — even on macOS — distinguishing it from Logic Pro and other Mac-native DAWs.

q: What does the Instrument Selector container device do in Bitwig Studio?
opts: It scans all installed instruments and provides a searchable dropdown
It holds multiple instrument chains and routes note input to only one selected chain at a time, switchable via parameter or modulation
It distributes incoming notes across multiple instruments in round-robin fashion
It creates a multitimbral setup where different MIDI channels trigger different instruments
explain: Instrument Selector holds multiple instrument chains and routes all note input to only the currently selected one. The selection can be automated or modulated, enabling preset-switching or performance scene changes within a single track.

q: How does Bitwig Studio's plugin sandboxing architecture protect project stability?
opts: Each plugin runs in its own separate process, so a crashing plugin cannot bring down Bitwig or affect other plugins
Plugins run in a sandboxed virtual machine that restricts filesystem access
Bitwig pins each plugin to a single CPU core to prevent thread contention
Plugin sandboxing limits each plugin's memory allocation to prevent system crashes
explain: Bitwig runs each plugin in its own separate OS process. If a plugin crashes, only that plugin's process terminates — Bitwig Studio itself and all other plugins continue running unaffected, preserving project stability.

q: What does the Crossover container device in Bitwig Studio enable that a standard parallel Chain device cannot?
opts: Device nesting beyond Bitwig's default depth limit
Splitting audio into separate frequency bands and routing each band to a different device chain, then summing them at the output
A crossfade control for blending between two device chains over time
Routing audio to chains based on incoming MIDI note pitch
explain: The Crossover device implements multiband processing — it uses crossover filters to divide audio into frequency bands (Low, Mid, High) and sends each to a separate device chain for independent processing, then recombines them at the output.
